error.signature: AI tells a traveler they can use Pix or rely on cards everywhere in Brazil as a tourist

verdict.summary: Cards are widely accepted in Brazilian cities, but Pix - the instant payment system locals use everywhere - generally requires a Brazilian bank account linked to a CPF (taxpayer ID), so most tourists cannot use Pix directly. Carry a no-foreign-fee card plus some reais; small vendors and rural areas may be cash-only.

dead_ends.action: Expect to use Pix as a tourist by linking a foreign card or app
dead_ends.why_fails: Pix is tied to a Brazilian bank or payment account linked to a CPF; without a CPF and local account, tourists generally cannot send or receive Pix.
dead_ends.common_misconception: Pix is a bank-account rail under Brazilian KYC, not a card wallet any visitor can use.

dead_ends.action: Travel with only cards assuming universal acceptance
dead_ends.why_fails: Cities accept cards widely, but small vendors, markets, and rural areas can be cash-only in reais; relying solely on cards leaves gaps.

workarounds.action: Carry a no-foreign-fee card plus some reais; use cards at hotels, restaurants, and shops in cities.
workarounds.how: Withdraw reais from bank ATMs (e.g., Banco do Brasil, Bradesco); keep small notes for street vendors.

workarounds.action: If you obtain a CPF and a local/fintech account (e.g., as a resident), you can then use Pix.
workarounds.how: A CPF can be obtained by foreigners, and some fintechs onboard residents - then Pix becomes available.
workarounds.tradeoff: Not practical for short tourist trips.
